subject: maths
title: Some Applications of Trigonometry

nodes.title: Solve a heights-and-distances problem with trigonometry
nodes.brief: Given a real-world height/distance situation, model it as right triangle(s), pick the correct trig ratio, and solve for the unknown length.
nodes.quote: The height or length of an object or the distance between two distant objects can be determined with the help of trigonometric ratios.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read the scene and identify what is unknown.
A word problem describes a real situation: a tower, a ladder, a ship, a flagstaff. Your first job is to name the asked quantity (a height, a distance, a length of rope) and decide which segments of the figure correspond to it. The rest of the method is mechanical once you know what you need.

PART 2 — Draw and label the right triangle(s).
Sketch the scene: vertical object as one leg, ground (or horizontal) as the other leg, right angle at the base. Mark the known angle of elevation or depression, the given side length, and the unknown. Composite problems (flagstaff, two ships, shadow) produce two triangles sharing a common side — label both.

PART 3 — Choose the trig ratio and solve.
Select the ratio that links exactly the known side to the unknown side (tan when opposite and adjacent, sin/cos when the hypotenuse is involved). Write the equation, substitute the exact value (tan60=sqrt(3), tan30=1/sqrt(3), sin60=sqrt(3)/2), isolate the unknown, and finally combine sub-lengths or add the observer's height if needed. Example: tower seen at 60 degrees from 15 m away gives tan(60)=height/15, so height = 15*sqrt(3) m.
keyMoves: Read the situation and decide what length is asked
Draw the right triangle(s) and label known angle and known side
Choose the trig ratio that links the unknown to the known
Set up and solve the equation, then add/subtract auxiliary lengths
misconceptions: Trying to solve without drawing/labelling the triangle
Forgetting to add the observer's height or a base length to the triangle's answer

nodes.title: Draw and label the right triangle from a word problem
nodes.brief: Translate the verbal scene into a right triangle, marking the right angle, the given angle, the known side, and the unknown side.
nodes.quote: First let us draw a simple diagram to represent the problem (see Fig. 9.4). Here AB represents the tower, CB is the distance of the point from the tower and ∠ ACB is the angle of elevation.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the physical setup.
Every heights-and-distances problem describes a physical scene: a vertical object (tower, pole, chimney), a horizontal ground distance, and an observer at some point. Before writing any equation, decide which segment is the vertical leg, which is the horizontal leg, and where the right angle sits (always at the base of the vertical object on level ground).

PART 2 — Sketch and label the triangle.
Draw the vertical side AB (object), the horizontal side BC (ground distance), and the right angle at B. Mark the observer's position (C or A depending on the problem), write in the given angle at that vertex, and label the unknown side with a letter. Example: tower AB, observer at C, angle ACB = 60 degrees, BC = 15 m — the triangle is a right triangle with the right angle at B.

PART 3 — Identify opposite and adjacent relative to the given angle.
From the observer's vertex, the opposite side is the one across the triangle (usually the height) and the adjacent is the side next to the angle (usually the ground distance). Getting this labelling correct determines which trig ratio to apply next.
keyMoves: Represent the vertical object as one leg and ground distance as the other leg
Mark the right angle at the base
Identify the given angle's vertex and the side asked for
misconceptions: Placing the right angle at the wrong vertex
Mislabelling which side is opposite vs adjacent to the given angle

nodes.title: Identify the angle of elevation
nodes.brief: Recognise the angle of elevation as the angle the upward line of sight makes with the horizontal.
nodes.quote: The angle of elevation of the point viewed is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when it is above the horizontal level, i.e., the case when we raise our head to look at the object
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What is the angle of elevation?
When an observer looks up at an object (the top of a tower, a kite, a plane), the line of sight rises above the horizontal. The angle between the horizontal at the observer's eye and this upward line of sight is called the angle of elevation. It is always measured at the observer's position, and it is always above the horizontal.

PART 2 — Locating it in the triangle.
In the right triangle, the angle of elevation sits at the observer's vertex (the far end of the horizontal base). The horizontal base is the adjacent side, and the height of the object is the opposite side. Example: an observer on the ground looks up at a tower top; the angle between the ground (horizontal) and the line to the top is 60 degrees — that is the angle of elevation, and it sits at the base vertex C of triangle ABC.

PART 3 — Common mistake to avoid.
Do not measure the angle from the vertical — that gives the complement (30 degrees when the elevation is 60 degrees). The reference is always the horizontal.
keyMoves: Locate the line of sight from observer to the high point
Measure its angle above the horizontal
misconceptions: Confusing angle of elevation with angle of depression
Measuring the angle from the vertical instead of the horizontal

nodes.title: Identify the angle of depression
nodes.brief: Recognise the angle of depression as the angle the downward line of sight makes with the horizontal.
nodes.quote: the angle of depression of a point on the object being viewed is the angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when the point is below the horizontal level, i.e., the case when we lower our head to look at the point being viewed
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What is the angle of depression?
When an observer is at a height (top of a lighthouse, roof of a building, cliff) and looks down at a point below, the line of sight goes below the horizontal. The angle between the horizontal at the observer's eye and this downward line of sight is the angle of depression. Like the elevation angle, it is measured from the horizontal — but downward.

PART 2 — Drawing it correctly.
Draw a horizontal line at the observer's high position. The line of sight goes down to the object on the ground. The angle between the horizontal and this line of sight, measured downward, is the angle of depression. Example: a lighthouse keeper at height 75 m looks down at a ship; the line of sight drops 30 degrees below the horizontal — the angle of depression is 30 degrees.

PART 3 — Why the horizontal reference matters.
The angle of depression is NOT the angle inside the right triangle at the top vertex directly — to use it in the triangle you must transfer it using alternate angles (covered in the next node). Always draw the horizontal at the top to avoid placing it in the wrong position.
keyMoves: Locate the horizontal at the observer's eye
Measure the angle down to the line of sight
misconceptions: Calling a downward-look angle an angle of elevation
Forgetting the horizontal reference line at the top

nodes.title: Convert an angle of depression to an angle inside the triangle (alternate angles)
nodes.brief: Use that the angle of depression equals the alternate interior angle at the lower point because the two horizontals are parallel.
nodes.quote: Observe that PB is a transversal to the parallel lines PQ and BD. Therefore, ∠ QPB and ∠ PBD are alternate angles, and so are equal. So, ∠ PBD = 30°.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why you need to transfer the angle.
The angle of depression is measured at the top (observer's eye) between the upper horizontal and the line of sight going down. But in the right triangle the useful angle is at the bottom vertex (the observed point). These two angles are equal because the upper horizontal and the ground are parallel lines cut by the line of sight as a transversal.

PART 2 — Alternate interior angles theorem.
When a transversal crosses two parallel lines, alternate interior angles are equal. Here: the upper horizontal at P and the ground BD are parallel; the line of sight PB is the transversal; angle QPB (depression at top) and angle PBD (angle at bottom inside triangle) are alternate interior angles, so angle PBD = angle QPB. Now you can use angle PBD directly inside the right triangle.

PART 3 — Worked example.
An observer at P, 75 m high, sees a ship at B with angle of depression 30 degrees. Horizontal PQ is parallel to ground BD. So angle PBD = 30 degrees (alternate angles). In right triangle PBD: tan(30) = PD/BD = 75/BD, giving BD = 75/tan(30) = 75*sqrt(3) m.
keyMoves: Recognise the two horizontals are parallel
Treat the line of sight as a transversal
Set the bottom angle equal to the depression angle
misconceptions: Placing the depression angle directly inside the triangle at the top without transferring
Not recognising the alternate-angle equality

nodes.title: Choose tangent when opposite and adjacent sides are involved
nodes.brief: Pick tan of the known angle when the unknown and known sides are the side opposite and the side adjacent to that angle.
nodes.quote: tan A = BC/AB    or    cot A = AB/BC, which on solving would give us BC.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When to pick tangent.
Tangent = opposite / adjacent. Choose tan when neither the unknown nor the known side is the hypotenuse — that is, when the problem involves a height (opposite the angle) and a horizontal ground distance (adjacent to the angle). If the hypotenuse appears, switch to sin or cos.

PART 2 — Applying tan in a tower problem.
Tower AB, observer at C, angle ACB = 60 degrees, BC = 15 m (adjacent), AB = ? (opposite). tan(60) = AB/BC, so sqrt(3) = AB/15, giving AB = 15*sqrt(3) m. The hypotenuse AC never appears and is never needed.

PART 3 — Using cot for the reverse case.
If the height is known and the base is unknown, you can use cot = adjacent/opposite instead of inverting tan. cot(60) = BC/AB = 1/sqrt(3), so BC = AB/sqrt(3). Both tan and cot are valid; cot avoids the fraction-flip step.
keyMoves: Identify opposite and adjacent sides relative to the known angle
Use tan = opposite/adjacent to link them
misconceptions: Using sin or cos when no hypotenuse is involved
Swapping opposite and adjacent in the ratio

nodes.title: Choose sine when opposite side and hypotenuse are involved
nodes.brief: Pick sin of the known angle when the relevant sides are the side opposite the angle and the hypotenuse.
nodes.quote:
Now, can you think which trigonometric ratio should we consider?

It should be sin 60°.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When to pick sine.
Sine = opposite / hypotenuse. Choose sin when the two relevant sides are the side opposite the known angle and the hypotenuse (the slant side — a ladder, a rope, a wire). If neither side is the hypotenuse, use tan instead.

PART 2 — Ladder problem example.
A ladder BC leans against a vertical pole BD. The ladder makes angle 60 degrees with the ground. The vertical height BD = 3.7 m (opposite to 60 degrees) is known; the ladder length BC (hypotenuse) is unknown. sin(60) = BD/BC = 3.7/BC. So BC = 3.7/sin(60) = 3.7/(sqrt(3)/2) = 7.4/sqrt(3) = 7.4*sqrt(3)/3 ≈ 4.28 m.

PART 3 — Why not tan here?
tan(60) = BD/DC gives the horizontal foot distance DC, not the ladder length. The ladder is the hypotenuse connecting the top of the pole to the foot on the ground, so sin is the correct ratio for its length.
keyMoves: Note the hypotenuse is one of the two relevant sides
Pair it with the opposite side via sin = opposite/hypotenuse
misconceptions: Using tan when the hypotenuse is actually the unknown
Confusing sine (opposite) with cosine (adjacent)

nodes.title: Recall the trig ratio value of a standard angle
nodes.brief: Substitute the exact value of sin, cos, tan, or cot at 30°, 45°, or 60°.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The standard angle table for this chapter.
Every problem in this chapter uses an angle of 30, 45, or 60 degrees. Their exact trig values are: tan(30)=1/sqrt(3), sin(30)=1/2, cos(30)=sqrt(3)/2; tan(45)=1, sin(45)=1/sqrt(2), cos(45)=1/sqrt(2); tan(60)=sqrt(3), sin(60)=sqrt(3)/2, cos(60)=1/2. The reciprocals: cot(30)=sqrt(3), cot(45)=1, cot(60)=1/sqrt(3).

PART 2 — Memory strategy.
triangle with angles 30-60-90 has sides 1 : sqrt(3) : 2 (short : medium : hypotenuse). So tan(30)=1/sqrt(3), tan(60)=sqrt(3). The 45-45-90 triangle has sides 1:1:sqrt(2), giving tan(45)=1.

PART 3 — Example substitution.
A problem sets up sqrt(3) = AB/15 from tan(60). Substituting the exact value sqrt(3) (not the decimal 1.73) keeps the answer exact: AB = 15*sqrt(3). Only convert to a decimal at the very end, and only when the problem asks for an approximate answer.
keyMoves: Identify the standard angle (30, 45, 60)
Recall the exact value of the required ratio
misconceptions: Mixing up tan30 (1/√3) with tan60 (√3)
Using a decimal approximation prematurely

nodes.title: Set up the trig equation relating the unknown to known values
nodes.brief: Write the equation 'ratio of known angle = (one side)/(other side)' with the known length substituted.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Translate the geometry into an equation.
Once you have identified the angle, the known side, and the unknown side, write: ratio(angle) = side_A / side_B. Then substitute the exact value of the trig ratio on the left and the known length on the correct side of the fraction. The result is a one-variable equation.

PART 2 — Worked example.
Triangle with angle 60 degrees at C, BC = 15 m (adjacent, known), AB = h (opposite, unknown). tan(60) = AB/BC gives sqrt(3) = h/15. Substituting tan(60)=sqrt(3) and BC=15 produces the equation sqrt(3) = h/15, which has one unknown h.

PART 3 — Common errors.
Inverting the ratio gives sqrt(3) = 15/h instead of h/15 — always double-check which side is opposite and which is adjacent before writing the fraction. Also, substitute the known length into the right position; putting 15 in the numerator when it is the adjacent would give the wrong equation.
keyMoves: Write ratio(angle) = side/side
Substitute the standard-angle value and the known length
misconceptions: Inverting the side ratio
Substituting the known length into the wrong position

nodes.title: Solve the trig equation for the unknown side
nodes.brief: Rearrange the equation (multiply/divide) to isolate the unknown length.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Isolating a numerator unknown.
If the equation is ratio_value = unknown/known, multiply both sides by known: unknown = ratio_value * known. Example: sqrt(3) = AB/15 gives AB = 15*sqrt(3). This is the most common form in this chapter.

PART 2 — Isolating a denominator unknown.
If the equation is ratio_value = known/unknown (e.g., sin(60) = 3.7/BC), cross-multiply: unknown = known/ratio_value. Example: sqrt(3)/2 = 3.7/BC gives BC = 3.7*2/sqrt(3) = 7.4/sqrt(3).

PART 3 — Keep surds exact until the final step.
Leave answers like 15*sqrt(3) or 7.4/sqrt(3) in exact form throughout. Only convert to a decimal (using sqrt(3) approx 1.732) at the very end when an approximate answer is required.
keyMoves: Cross-multiply or multiply through to clear the fraction
Isolate the unknown
misconceptions: Dividing instead of multiplying
Dropping the surd factor

nodes.title: Compute a hypotenuse length using sine
nodes.brief: From sin(angle) = opposite/hypotenuse with the opposite known, solve for the hypotenuse.
nodes.quote:
So,    BD/BC = sin 60°    or    3.7/BC = √3/2

Therefore,    BC = (3.7 × 2)/√3 = 4.28 m (approx.)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Setting up the sine equation for the hypotenuse.
When the hypotenuse is the unknown, sin(angle) = opposite/hypotenuse rearranges to hypotenuse = opposite/sin(angle). Example: a ladder BC leans against a pole of height BD = 3.7 m at angle 60 degrees. sin(60) = BD/BC gives BC = BD/sin(60) = 3.7/(sqrt(3)/2).

PART 2 — Simplifying the fraction.
3.7 / (sqrt(3)/2) = 3.7 * 2/sqrt(3) = 7.4/sqrt(3). Rationalise: 7.4/sqrt(3) = 7.4*sqrt(3)/3 = (7.4*1.732)/3 approx 12.82/3 approx 4.28 m. So the ladder length is approximately 4.28 m.

PART 3 — Why rationalise or approximate?
Leaving 7.4/sqrt(3) is exact but the problem says take sqrt(3) = 1.732, so substitute and divide: 7.4*1.732/3 = 12.82/3 = 4.27 m (rounding to 4.28 m using more decimal places of sqrt(3)).
keyMoves: Cross-multiply to move the hypotenuse to the numerator
Approximate the surd to a decimal
misconceptions: Leaving the hypotenuse in the denominator
Using √3/2 upside down

nodes.title: Compute the horizontal base using cot
nodes.brief: Use cot(angle) = adjacent/opposite to find the horizontal distance from the foot.
nodes.quote:
Now,    DC/BD = cot 60° = 1/√3

i.e.,    DC = 3.7/√3 = 2.14 m (approx.)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When cot is the right choice.
cot(angle) = adjacent/opposite. Use cot when you know the opposite side (height) and want the adjacent side (horizontal base). This avoids writing tan and then inverting: cot(60) = 1/sqrt(3) directly gives the ratio adjacent/opposite.

PART 2 — Worked example.
Pole height BD = 3.7 m (opposite to 60 degrees at C). Foot of ladder D is distance DC from base of pole (adjacent). cot(60) = DC/BD = 1/sqrt(3), so DC = BD/sqrt(3) = 3.7/sqrt(3). Rationalise: DC = 3.7*sqrt(3)/3 = (3.7*1.732)/3 approx 6.408/3 approx 2.14 m.

PART 3 — Relation to tan.
cot(60) = 1/tan(60) = 1/sqrt(3). You could equally write tan(60) = BD/DC = 3.7/DC, so DC = 3.7/sqrt(3) — same result. cot is just the shorthand; both approaches are correct.
keyMoves: Identify adjacent and opposite relative to the angle
Apply cot = adjacent/opposite and solve
misconceptions: Confusing cot with tan
Forgetting cot60 = 1/√3

nodes.title: Add the observer's height to the triangle result
nodes.brief: When the angle is measured from an observer's eye, add the eye height to the computed leg to get the full object height.
nodes.quote: We have    AB = AE + BE = AE + 1.5
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why the triangle does not give the full height.
When an observer of height 1.5 m looks up at a chimney, the line of sight starts at eye level (1.5 m above the ground), not at the ground. The right triangle gives only the part of the chimney above eye level — call it AE. The total chimney height AB = AE + BE = AE + 1.5.

PART 2 — Worked example.
Observer is 1.5 m tall, 28.5 m from the base of a chimney. Angle of elevation of the top = 45 degrees. tan(45) = AE/28.5, so 1 = AE/28.5, giving AE = 28.5 m. Total height = AE + 1.5 = 28.5 + 1.5 = 30 m.

PART 3 — General rule.
Whenever the problem says the angle is observed from a person's eye, or gives the observer's height, add that height to the triangle result. If the problem says the observer stands at ground level with eye at height h, add h to the computed leg.
keyMoves: Recognise the triangle's vertical leg starts at eye level
Add observer height to the leg
misconceptions: Reporting only the triangle leg as the full height
Subtracting instead of adding the observer height

nodes.title: Model a problem with two right triangles sharing a side
nodes.brief: When two angles are given for one base or one object, build two right triangles sharing a common side and write an equation from each.
nodes.quote: Note that there are two right triangles PAB and PAD. We are required to find the length of the flagstaff, i.e., DB and the distance of the building from the point P, i.e., PA.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognising the two-triangle structure.
Some problems give two angles from the same base point to two different heights (bottom and top of a flagstaff), or two angles from two different points to the same height. Each angle-height pair defines one right triangle. The two triangles share a common side — the base PA or the common height AB — which is the algebraic link between the two equations.

PART 2 — Setting up the two equations.
Label the shared side as a variable (say x for the base PA). Write a trig equation for each triangle: for the lower angle, tan(lower) = AB/PA = h1/x; for the upper angle, tan(upper) = (AB+DB)/PA = h2/x. Two equations, two unknowns — solve by substitution or division.

PART 3 — Example (flagstaff).
P is 30 m from the base. Angle to bottom of flag (B) is 30 degrees, angle to top (A) is 60 degrees. Triangle PAB: tan(60) = AB/30, so AB = 30*sqrt(3). Triangle PAD: tan(30) = DB/30 = 1/sqrt(3), so ... wait — actually tan(30) relates to the angle to the top of the pole below the flag. Set up both triangles carefully with the shared base PA = 30 m.
keyMoves: Identify the shared side between the two triangles
Write a separate trig equation for each angle
misconceptions: Treating the two triangles as independent (ignoring the shared side)
Mislabelling which angle belongs to which triangle

nodes.title: Solve two trig equations by substitution
nodes.brief: Eliminate one unknown by substituting one trig equation into the other and solve for the remaining unknown.
nodes.quote:
From (1), we have    h = x√3

Putting this value in (2), we get (x√3)√3 = x + 40, i.e., 3x = x + 40

i.e.,    x = 20
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Express one variable from the first equation.
In the shadow problem, a tree of height h casts a shadow of length x m on flat ground. The sun's angle of elevation is 60 degrees from the tip of the shadow. From triangle 1: tan(60) = h/x gives h = x*sqrt(3). This expresses h in terms of x.

PART 2 — Substitute into the second equation.
A second angle of 30 degrees is observed from a point 40 m beyond the shadow tip: tan(30) = h/(x+40), so 1/sqrt(3) = h/(x+40), giving h = (x+40)/sqrt(3). Now substitute h = x*sqrt(3): x*sqrt(3) = (x+40)/sqrt(3). Multiply both sides by sqrt(3): x*(sqrt(3))^2 = x+40, so 3x = x+40, giving 2x = 40, x = 20.

PART 3 — Recover both unknowns and verify.
x = 20 m (shadow length), h = 20*sqrt(3) m (tree height). Check: tan(60) = 20*sqrt(3)/20 = sqrt(3) -- correct; tan(30) = 20*sqrt(3)/60 = sqrt(3)/3 = 1/sqrt(3) -- correct. Note: sqrt(3)*sqrt(3) = 3, not sqrt(6); this is the most common algebra error in this step.
keyMoves: Express one variable from the first equation
Substitute into the second and simplify the surds
Solve the resulting linear equation
misconceptions: Algebra errors with √3·√3 = 3
Substituting into the wrong equation

nodes.title: Rationalise a surd denominator
nodes.brief: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate to remove a surd from the denominator.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When rationalisation is needed.
After solving two-triangle problems, the answer often has the form a/(sqrt(3)-1) or a/(sqrt(3)+1) — a surd in the denominator. This is not the simplest form. Rationalise by multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.

PART 2 — The conjugate trick.
The conjugate of (sqrt(3)-1) is (sqrt(3)+1). Their product is (sqrt(3))^2 - 1^2 = 3 - 1 = 2 (difference of squares). So: 8/(sqrt(3)-1) * (sqrt(3)+1)/(sqrt(3)+1) = 8*(sqrt(3)+1)/2 = 4*(sqrt(3)+1) m.

PART 3 — Worked step-by-step.
PD = 8/(sqrt(3)-1). Multiply: numerator = 8*(sqrt(3)+1); denominator = (sqrt(3)-1)*(sqrt(3)+1) = 3-1 = 2. So PD = 8*(sqrt(3)+1)/2 = 4*(sqrt(3)+1) = 4*sqrt(3)+4 m. If sqrt(3) = 1.732: PD = 4*1.732+4 = 6.928+4 = 10.928 m.
keyMoves: Multiply by conjugate over itself
Use (a−b)(a+b)=a²−b² in the denominator
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply the numerator too
Sign error in the conjugate

nodes.title: Approximate a surd answer to a decimal
nodes.brief: Substitute the given value of √3 (e.g. 1.732) to convert an exact surd answer to a decimal.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When to convert to a decimal.
Problems that say 'Use sqrt(3) = 1.732' or 'Take sqrt(2) = 1.414' are telling you to give a decimal answer. Keep the exact surd form through all algebra; only substitute in the last step.

PART 2 — Worked example.
Exact answer: x = 10*(sqrt(3)-1). Substitute sqrt(3) = 1.732: x = 10*(1.732-1) = 10*0.732 = 7.32 m. Do the subtraction first, then multiply — reversing the order gives the same result but the subtraction-then-multiply order is less error-prone.

PART 3 — Common mistakes.
Using sqrt(3) = 1.73 (only 2 decimal places) gives 10*0.73 = 7.3 m — slightly off. Always use the value given in the problem (usually 1.732). Also watch the order: 10*(sqrt(3)-1) is NOT the same as 10*sqrt(3)-1 = 17.32-1 = 16.32; the parentheses must be applied first.
keyMoves: Substitute the supplied value of √3
Carry out the arithmetic carefully
misconceptions: Using a wrong value for √3
Arithmetic slip in subtraction/multiplication

nodes.title: Combine segment lengths to get the asked quantity
nodes.brief: Add or subtract the computed pieces (sub-lengths) to produce the final asked height/width/distance.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — When the answer is a sum of two sub-lengths.
In river-width or two-point problems, the total distance you need is broken into two parts by the point directly below a common object. Each part comes from one right triangle. The final answer is the sum (or difference) of the two computed lengths.

PART 2 — Worked example (river width).
A tree on a river bank is observed from two points A and B on the opposite bank. C is the foot of the tree, D is the point on the bank directly opposite. CD is perpendicular to AB. From A: angle = 60 degrees, so AD = CD/tan(60) = CD/sqrt(3). From B: angle = 30 degrees, so BD = CD/tan(30) = CD*sqrt(3). If CD = 3 m: AD = 3/sqrt(3) = sqrt(3) m, BD = 3*sqrt(3) m. Width AB = AD + BD = sqrt(3) + 3*sqrt(3) = 4*sqrt(3) m.

PART 3 — Factoring the final answer.
When both sub-lengths share a common factor, factor it out: e.g., AD = 3 m, BD = 3*sqrt(3) m gives AB = 3+3*sqrt(3) = 3*(1+sqrt(3)) m. This compact form is the expected final answer.
keyMoves: Identify which sub-lengths add to the target
Sum (or subtract) and factor if useful
misconceptions: Reporting only one sub-length
Adding lengths that should be subtracted

nodes.title: Use that a 45° right triangle has equal legs
nodes.brief: Recognise that when the angle is 45°, the opposite and adjacent legs are equal (tan45 = 1).
nodes.quote: Also, in right ∆ PBD, ∠ B = 45°. So, BD = PD = 3 m.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The 45-degree special case.
In a right triangle with one angle equal to 45 degrees, the other non-right angle is also 45 degrees. This means the triangle is isosceles: the two legs (opposite and adjacent) are equal. Equivalently, tan(45) = 1, so opposite/adjacent = 1, giving opposite = adjacent.

PART 2 — Using it to skip algebra.
When you see a 45-degree angle in a right triangle, immediately write: opposite leg = adjacent leg. Example: in right triangle PBD with angle B = 45 degrees and one leg BD = 3 m, the other leg PD = BD = 3 m. No equation to solve.

PART 3 — Worked problem context.
A tower PD of unknown height is observed from point B at an angle of elevation of 45 degrees. BD = 3 m (given horizontal distance). tan(45) = PD/BD gives PD = BD*1 = 3 m. The equal-legs shortcut saves one step: you can simply write PD = 3 m directly.
keyMoves: Note the angle is 45°
Set the opposite leg equal to the adjacent leg
misconceptions: Not realising tan45 = 1 forces equal legs
Still setting up a full ratio unnecessarily

narrative:
## What was read

I re-read the full segmented chapter text `jemh109.txt` (SEG 0 through SEG 4: 9.1 Heights and Distances with Examples 1-7, EXERCISE 9.1, and the 9.2 Summary) and the shared `_maths-exams.txt` (CBSE Standard + Basic SQP and Marking Schemes). I audited the DRAFT line-by-line, opening each tagged segment and locating each quote verbatim.

## Corrections made in this review

Three citation SEC errors were found and fixed (the #1 failure mode):
1. `solve-heights-and-distances-problem` (goal): quote "The height or length of an object ... determined with the help of trigonometric ratios." was tagged sec 0 but lives ONLY at line 267 in SEG 4 (the Summary). Fixed sec 0 → 4.
2. `set-up-two-triangles-shared-side`: quote "Note that there are two right triangles PAB and PAD..." (Example 4, line 116) is in SEG 1, not SEG 0. Fixed sec 0 → 1.
3. `solve-simultaneous-trig-equations`: quote "From (1), we have h = x√3 ... x = 20" (Example 5, lines 160-164) is in SEG 1, not SEG 0. Fixed sec 0 → 1.

All other 16 node quotes and all 19 gate quotes were located character-for-character in their tagged segments and left unchanged. ## Independent gate answer verification

I re-solved every gate from first principles: 15·tan60 = 15√3; 3·tan30 = 3/√3 (= √3, matches marking scheme option C at exam line 340); √3 = AB/15; AB = 15√3; 28.5+1.5 = 30; 3x = x+40 ⇒ x = 20; tan30=10/AP ⇒ AP = 10√3; 10(1.732−1) = 7.32; 8/(√3−1) = 4(√3+1); 3+3√3 = 3(1+√3); BD = PD = 3; 7.4/1.73 ≈ 4.28; 3.7/1.73 ≈ 2.14; right angle at B; sin 60°; tan A = BC/AB; ∠PBD = 30°; elevation; depression. Every expected value is mathematically correct and self-consistent with its grader (cas/numeric/mcq option string).

absentNotes: The chapter does NOT teach or prove the underlying trigonometric ratios themselves (sin/cos/tan definitions), the Pythagoras theorem, or the alternate-interior-angles theorem — these are assumed from prior chapters and so are not nodes. It does not cover: angles other than 30°/45°/60°; bearings or compass directions; 3-D applications; problems requiring the sine/cosine rule (not in Class 10 scope); or any calculus/rate-of-change reasoning (Exercise Q15 asks for time-to-reach but its solution/answer is not given in the source, so no rate node was created). The Summary (SEG 4) only restates definitions and the one-line claim that heights/distances can be found with trig ratios — no new skill. Exercise 9.1 problems (SEG 3) are practice items with no stated answers in the source text, so although they exercise the same skills, none were turned into nodes or gates. The exam SQPs include trig-application problems (Standard Q34 balloon, Basic Q35 building-and-tower / lighthouse) that map onto the existing two-triangle and observer-height nodes; they were not added as separate nodes to avoid redundancy.
